title: SPORTING SUPERSTITIONS
subtitle: For questions 1–8, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
passage:
Most of (0) us have routines, habits and superstitions. We'll wear our lucky shirt to an interview, arrange our cutlery in a particular way or turn the oven off three times, just to [[1]] sure. In a competitive environment, in [[2]] athletes are all going for gold, years of training, drastic diets and intense coaching might just [[3]] be enough … so, sometimes, the Olympians turn [[4]] luck for extra support.

'Superstition is very common in sport most of all because of the uncertainty of the outcome,' says sports psychologist Dr George Sik. 'Athletes use it so they can rely on something else other [[5]] their own consciousness. Sport is generally based on repetition and superstition means being in control, adding a safety net. It affects the mind and not the body. If you convince [[6]] that you are in luck, you'll boost your confidence and tend to perform better. And if you fail, you can always blame it [[7]] the luck.'

So, [[8]] they may know deep down that a 'lucky' pair of socks won't take them over the line any faster, many athletes stick to their rituals for that little bit of confidence, belief and a sense of security.

explanations.1: Just to make sure is a fixed phrase: you repeat a small action to feel certain everything is safe or correct.
explanations.2: After a place/context noun (environment / situation), in which introduces a relative clause about that setting — here, where athletes compete for gold.
explanations.3: Might just not be enough — not negates be enough; training alone is insufficient, so athletes add superstition.
explanations.4: Turn to (someone or something) means go to it for help or support — here, they rely on luck.
explanations.5: Other than means apart from or except — something besides their own consciousness.
explanations.6: Convince yourself — reflexive with you: persuade yourself (that you are lucky).
explanations.7: Blame it on (something) needs the preposition on — attribute failure to luck.
explanations.8: Although / though introduces concession: even though they know rationally socks don't make them faster, they still follow rituals.
